message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** Find the best available brand name for a software product, and verify availability for each finalist. 

THE PRODUCT: an autonomous "deep research" AI agent/pipeline. It fans out web + academic searches across many sources, then writes a long-form research report (e.g. literature reviews, market/industry analyses) in which EVERY claim is traced back to a real, verifiable citation. Its core differentiator vs competitors is FAITHFULNESS and RIGOR — a built-in verifier repairs/re-grounds each sentence to source evidence so the report does not hallucinate. Think of it as "a research analyst that never makes up a fact." Positioning themes to draw from: truth / veracity / faithfulness / proof, depth / synthesis / research, insight / clarity / illumination, and (secondarily) autonomous agentic speed.

CURRENT NAME TO REPLACE: "Polaris" (rejected — too generic, and heavily trademarked: Polaris Inc. snowmobiles, Polaris missiles, many software products). AVOID this and the whole cliché family of star/compass/navigation/"north star" names.

NAMING STYLE: open — prefer short (2-3 syllables), pronounceable, brandable names. Coined/abstract words (like Vercel, Cohere, Anthropic, Kadoa) are welcome because they are the most ownable; obscure real words or Greek/Latin/other-language roots with a truth/insight meaning are also welcome. Avoid the crowded, already-taken names in the AI-research space — explicitly check and EXCLUDE names already used by: Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Undermind, Scite, You.com, Genspark, Storm/STORM (Stanford), Ai2/OpenScholar, Scholarcy, Semantic Scholar, Deep Research (OpenAI/Google/Perplexity feature names), Glean, Hebbia, Vespa, and any obvious near-duplicates.

DELIVERABLE: a RANKED shortlist of 8-12 candidate names. For EACH candidate provide:
  1. The name, its pronunciation, and a 1-2 sentence meaning/rationale tying it to the truth/rigor/deep-research positioning.
  2. GitHub availability: is the org/username taken? (check github.com/<name>).
  3. Trademark risk: search USPTO TESS and EUIPO for live marks in the software/AI classes (Nice class 9 and 42); flag any conflict, note if clear.
  4. Domain availability: is <name>.ai and/or <name>.com registered/parked/available? Note approximate acquisition feasibility.
  5. Social handle: is @<name> (or a close variant) free on X/Twitter?
  6. A risk flag: GREEN (all clear), YELLOW (minor/partial conflict), or RED (real conflict) with one-line reason.
Then give a clear TOP RECOMMENDATION (1-2 names) that best balances meaning + full availability across all four channels, and explain why. Prioritize candidates that are GREEN on ALL of: GitHub org, trademark, at least one of .ai/.com, and a social handle. It is better to return fewer names that are genuinely fully-available than many that are taken.

24 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] Instant Domain Search checks domain availability in real-time across hundreds of TLDs, returning instant results as the user types.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://instantdomainsearch.com/ (primary)
Quote: "Real-time availability checking across hundreds of TLDs"
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source (instantdomainsearch.com) directly supports both parts of the claim. WebFetch of the homepage returned "Real-time domain availability search with instant results as you type" and "search across hundreds of extensions" / "800+ TLD extensions." This confirms (a) real-time checking as the user types and (b) coverage across hundreds of TLDs. The claim's "hundreds of TLDs" is accurate and, if anything, conservative versus the site's stated 800+. This is a self-verifiable functional product feature (not an extraordinary claim), sourced from the primary vendor site, with current 2026-dated supporting pages (e.g., "Domain Extensions List ... (2026)"). No contradicting or qualifying evidence found. Only minor nuance: the tool is a checker/comparison front-end aggregating registrar data, but that does not undercut the real-time-as-you-type or hundreds-of-TLDs claim.

### [1] The tool supports both .ai and .com extensions (the two TLDs required by the naming task), with dedicated pages per TLD.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://instantdomainsearch.com/ (primary)
Quote: "hundreds of extensions"
Verifier evidence (high): Confirmed via primary source. instantdomainsearch.com supports 800+ TLDs including both .ai and .com (the two TLDs the naming task requires); the "hundreds of extensions" quote if anything understates coverage. Dedicated per-TLD pages are verified live: the dedicated .ai page at instantdomainsearch.com/domain-extensions/ai loads with .ai-specific search ("Search .ai domains"), 5-registrar pricing, and 2026 registry stats (990,913 registered .ai domains). Search results confirm the same /domain-extensions/{tld} pattern exists for .com and other extensions. This is a mundane operational claim about a domain-search tool, so primary-source verification is sufficient; data is current (2026) and no contradicting evidence exists.

### [2] The Latin root 'ver' means 'truth' or 'true', making it a semantically direct root for naming a product positioned on faithfulness/veracity.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://membean.com/roots/ver-truth (secondary)
Quote: "The Latin root word ver means “truth” or “true.”"
Verifier evidence (high): Multiple independent, high-quality sources corroborate the claim. Etymonline (the standard online etymology dictionary) and Wiktionary confirm the Latin adjective 'verus' means 'true' (from PIE *were-o- 'true, trustworthy') and is the direct root of veracity (via verax 'truthful' > veritas 'truthfulness'), verity, verify, veritas, verdict, veridical, and verisimilitude. The Membean quote ('the Latin root word ver means truth or true') accurately reflects this. This is a settled, uncontroversial linguistic fact, not a marketing or time-sensitive claim, and no source disputes it. The root is a semantically direct fit for a truth/faithfulness/veracity positioning.

### [3] "Veritas" is a Latin word meaning literally 'truth, truthfulness, that which is true', directly supporting a truth/faithfulness brand positioning.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.etymonline.com/word/veritas (secondary)
Quote: "veritas is "literally 'truth, truthfulness, that which is true.'""
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is corroborated by multiple independent authoritative sources, not just the etymonline secondary. Wiktionary lists "veritas" (from verus 'true' + -tas abstract-noun suffix) as meaning "truth, truthfulness." Merriam-Webster and Wikipedia both give "truth" and note Veritas as the Roman goddess/virtue of truth. Etymonline's own quote — "literally 'truth, truthfulness, that which is true'" — matches the claim verbatim. English descendants "verity" and "very" trace to it. This is elementary, uncontested dictionary-grade Latin etymology, so a secondary source is fully sufficient; there is no fast-moving or marketing dimension and no contradicting source exists. The word does directly denote truth/truthfulness, supporting the intended truth/faithfulness brand theme.

### [4] The word 'sooth' derives from Old English 'soð' meaning 'truth, reality, fact' and 'true, genuine, real; just, righteous' — directly supporting a truth/faithfulness brand positioning.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.etymonline.com/word/sooth (secondary)
Quote: "Old English soð meant "truth, reality, fact," as well as "true, genuine, real; just, righteous.""
Verifier evidence (high): The etymology claim is accurate and well-supported. The cited source (etymonline.com/word/sooth) states: "Old English soð 'truth, justice, righteousness, rectitude; reality, a true situation, certainty,' noun use of soð (adj.) 'true, genuine, real; just, righteous.'" This exactly matches the claim's supporting quote. Independent corroboration from Wiktionary confirms Old English "sōþ" meant "truth; true, actual, real," tracing to Proto-Germanic *sanþaz "true" and PIE *h₁s-ont- "being, real, true." Merriam-Webster, Dictionary.com, and Encyclopedia.com all agree. This is a standard, uncontroversial etymology — no dispute or qualification found in any credible source. The claim's meaning is faithful to the quote (not an overreach), the source quality (established etymological dictionaries) matches the claim strength, and etymology of a millennium-old word is not "outdated." NOTE (non-refuting branding caveat): "sooth" is archaic/obsolete in modern usage, surviving mainly in "soothsayer" (which connotes fortune-teller/prophet more than rigorous verified truth) — a positioning nuance, but it does not contradict the etymological fact asserted.

### [5] Undermind, Elicit, Consensus, Scholarcy, and STORM (Stanford) are all live, named products competing in the AI deep-research/analysis agent space as of mid-2026, confirming these names are taken and should be excluded from the naming shortlist.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://agentic.ai/best/research-deep-analysis (secondary)
Quote: "1. Agentic News 2. Undermind 3. SciSpace 4. Elicit 5. Scholarcy 6. Consensus 7. STORM by Stanford"
Verifier evidence (high): All five products independently confirmed live as of mid-2026 via primary/independent sources, not just the listicle: Elicit (elicit.com, "trusted by 5M+ researchers," 2026 pricing tiers); Consensus (consensus.app, 220M+ papers, ships a "Deep Search" research agent); Undermind (YC-backed, founded 2023 by Hartke & Ramette, autonomous literature-search agent, live free/$20 Pro pricing); Scholarcy (scholarcy.com, AI research summarizer, acquired by Texthelp Nov 2024 and still operating with 2026 reviews); STORM (Stanford OVAL, Wikipedia-style cited-report generator, 70,000+ users). The claim's operative point — these are real, named, live products in the research-AI space and thus their names are taken and should be excluded from the shortlist — is fully supported. Minor nuance that does not defeat the claim: Scholarcy is a summarizer rather than a fan-out agent, and STORM is a free research prototype rather than a commercial product; but both are unambiguously live named brands in the adjacent deep-research/analysis space, so the exclusion recommendation is correct. No contradicting source found; sources are current (multiple 2026-dated).

### [6] Nice Class 9 covers computer software and downloadable media, making it the relevant goods class for a software/AI product like the deep-research agent when searching trademarks.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://corsearch.com/content-library/blog/international-trademark-classes/ (secondary)
Quote: "recorded and downloadable media, computer software, blank digital or analogue recording and storage media"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is confirmed by primary and secondary sources. The official WIPO Nice Classification Class 9 heading includes "recorded and downloadable media, computer software, blank digital or analogue recording and storage media" — matching the supporting quote verbatim. WIPO's own text states Class 9 includes "all computer programs and software regardless of recording media or means of dissemination — software recorded on magnetic media or downloaded from a remote computer network." This is not an overreach: the claim says Class 9 is "the relevant goods class" for a software/AI product, and Class 9 is indeed the standard goods class for downloadable software. One minor qualification (not a refutation): for a purely SaaS/cloud-delivered agent, Class 42 ("software as a service; design and development of computer software") is the complementary and often more relevant SERVICES class — so a trademark search for this product should cover BOTH Class 9 and 42, which the parent research question already specifies. The claim itself is accurate as stated, current (WIPO Nice 11th ed.), and backed by a primary source (WIPO). Not a marketing claim.

### [7] Nice Class 42 covers software design/development and scientific/technological research services, making it the relevant services class for an AI research pipeline offered as a service.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://corsearch.com/content-library/blog/international-trademark-classes/ (secondary)
Quote: "Scientific and technological services and research and design relating thereto; industrial analysis and industrial research services; design and development of computer hardware and software."
Verifier evidence (high): The official USPTO/WIPO Nice Classification Class 42 heading reads verbatim: "Scientific and technological services and research and design relating thereto; industrial analysis, industrial research...; design and development of computer hardware and software." This directly confirms both prongs of the claim: (a) software design/development and (b) scientific/technological research services fall in Class 42. USPTO further lists SaaS, cloud computing, and "providing nondownloadable software" (MICROSOFT example) as canonical Class 42 services, confirming that an AI research pipeline offered AS A SERVICE belongs in Class 42. The Corsearch supporting quote matches the primary WIPO source. The claim is not an overreach — it correctly says "offered as a service" (Class 9 covers downloadable software products; Class 42 covers software-as-a-service). This is stable, uncontroversial trademark law consistent across Nice editions; no credible source disputes it.

### [8] Consensus positions itself specifically on evidence-based, claim-driven analysis — directly overlapping the faithfulness/rigor positioning of the product being named, so its name is already taken in the target space.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://paperguide.ai/blog/ai-tools-for-research/ (blog)
Quote: "Consensus specializes in evidence-based research by answering questions through claim-driven analysis"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by the quote and by stronger primary/secondary sources. Consensus.app's own site describes it as an "AI academic search engine" where "Every response includes citations, so you can trace each insight back to the original source," featuring a "Consensus Meter" that visualizes agreement/disagreement and a "Research Agent." Independent 2026 reviews (skywork.ai, effortlessacademic.com, TheAISelect) reinforce it is positioned on "evidence-based research," a "no hallucinations architecture" where "every claim is tied to a real paper," and "fabricated citations eliminated by design" — directly overlapping the product-being-named's faithfulness/rigor/citation-traceability positioning. Consensus is also explicitly on the exclude list in the research brief. The claim is current (sources dated 2025-2026), not marketing fluff (corroborated by university libraries and multiple independent reviewers), and matches the claim's strength. No credible contradicting evidence found.

### [9] Elicit, Perplexity, Semantic Scholar, Consensus, Scite, and Scholarcy are all named as leading AI research tools in 2025-2026, corroborating that these names are taken and must be excluded from the candidate list.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://paperguide.ai/blog/ai-tools-for-research/ (blog)
Quote: "Elicit streamlines literature reviews and data extraction by using AI to synthesize insights from academic papers"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim (these six are leading AI research tools, therefore taken and must be excluded) is overwhelmingly corroborated by many independent 2026 sources beyond the cited blog. Multiple sites list Elicit, Consensus, Scite, Perplexity, and Semantic Scholar together as top research tools: iatrox.com ("Best AI tools for medical research 2026: Elicit, Consensus, Semantic Scholar, Perplexity, and scite"), aitrove.ai ("Perplexity vs Consensus vs Elicit vs Scite"), cybernews.com, papersflow.ai, and buildmvpfast.com. Each is a real, established product with its own domain and user base: Elicit (elicit.com, 138M papers, API launched March 2026), Consensus (200M+ papers, "Consensus Meter"), Scite (Smart Citations), Perplexity ("Deep Research" mode), Semantic Scholar, and Scholarcy (scholarcy.com, $9.99/mo, own reviews). The claim is mundane and non-extraordinary (these names are demonstrably occupied), so a blog source plus this corroboration is more than sufficient. Sources are dated 2026 (current, not outdated). The one caveat — the supplied supporting quote only names Elicit, not all six — is a minor scope mismatch, but the full claim is independently and trivially verifiable, so it does not warrant refutation. These names must indeed be excluded from any candidate list.

### [10] Elicit, Consensus, Scite, and SciSpace are established, actively-marketed AI literature-review tools as of 2026 — confirming they are taken names in the AI-research space and must be excluded from the naming shortlist.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://paperguide.ai/blog/ai-tools-for-literature-review/ (blog)
Quote: "Elicit ... Strong citation grounding ... Consensus ... Evidence graded ... Scite ... Context graded Smart Citations; covers 1.2B+ citations"
Verifier evidence (high): All four tools are strongly confirmed as established, actively-marketed AI-research/literature tools as of 2026, by multiple independent sources beyond the single cited blog. Consensus: "10M+ users," "170+ university library partnerships," "$30M Series A funding raised in May 2026," Deep Search feature launched 2025 (theaiselect.com, okstate.edu library guide). Scite: "Scite MCP, launched in February 2026," AI Research Assistant with inline verified citations, $20/mo pricing (effortlessacademic.com, switchtools.io). SciSpace: 280M+ papers, Deep Review, AWS Marketplace listing (cybernews.com, arizona.edu LibGuide). Elicit: 138M+ papers, active 2026 reviews across many sites (aitoolranked.com, papersflow.ai). University library guides (Oklahoma State, Arizona) independently corroborate. Minor nuance: Elicit "does not generate literature review text" (it screens/extracts), so calling all four "literature-review tools" is slightly loose in category, but this does not affect the claim's operative conclusion — that these are taken/crowded names in the AI-research space to exclude from the shortlist. That conclusion is unambiguously correct.

### [11] The following AI research/literature-review product names are already in active commercial use as of early 2026 and are therefore taken in the target market: Cypris, Connected Papers, Research Rabbit, Litmaps, Iris.ai, Paper Digest, Publish or Perish, and CORE (in addition to Scholarcy and Semantic Scholar already on the exclude list).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.cypris.ai/insights/11-best-ai-tools-for-scientific-literature-review-in-2026 (blog)
Quote: "Connected Papers - Creates visual graphs showing citation networks... Research Rabbit - Discovers papers through citation relationships... Litmaps - Interactive citation maps... Iris.ai - AI research assistant... Paper Digest - Automated digests..."
Verifier evidence (high): All eight named products are confirmed as real, actively-used tools via INDEPENDENT sources (not just the cypris.ai blog): (1) Connected Papers, Research Rabbit, Litmaps — confirmed by Princeton, UMass, Bristol library guides and NIH/PMC (PMC10403115); Research Rabbit was acquired by Litmaps Nov 2025 and moved to freemium. (2) Iris.ai — founded 2015, Crunchbase profile, live at iris.ai. (3) Cypris — founded 2021, NYC, $5.3M Series A (Vocap, July 2024), customers incl. J&J; live at cypris.ai. (4) Publish or Perish — Harzing software since 2006, has a Wikipedia entry. (5) CORE (core.ac.uk) — world's largest OA aggregator, 400M+ records, documented in Nature (s41597-023-02208-w) and NIH/PMC. (6) Paper Digest — established AI paper-summary tool. All operate in the academic/research-tools market, so as names 'taken in the target space' the claim holds. Minor imprecision: they are not uniformly 'literature-review' products (some are citation-mapping, bibliometric, IP-intelligence, or OA-aggregation tools), but the core assertion — these names are in active commercial use and taken — is accurate and current.

### [12] The AI-research-assistant space already contains the products Elicit, Consensus, Scite (Scite.ai), and Semantic Scholar, all of which must be excluded as brand-name candidates per the research question.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.editage.us/blog/best-ai-research-assistants/ (blog)
Quote: "Elicit – Designed to "speed up systematic reviews"... Consensus – Intended to "get evidence-backed answers in clinical care"... Scite.ai – Built to "recheck references"... Semantic Scholar – Designed to "track down influential papers""
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly supported by the research question itself, which explicitly lists names to "check and EXCLUDE": "Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Undermind, Scite, You.com, ... Semantic Scholar..." So the exclusion mandate is literally stated in the prompt, not merely inferred from the blog. Independently, all four are confirmed as real, current (2025-2026) AI-research-assistant products by multiple sources: Elicit (elicit.com, "AI for scientific research"), Consensus ("evidence-backed answers from real papers", Consensus Meter), Scite (scite.ai, Smart Citations, 2M users, 280M+ articles, citation-backed answers), Semantic Scholar (200M+ papers, AI TLDRs). No contradicting evidence exists; these are canonical products in the space. Notably, Scite and Consensus are functional near-twins of the described product (claim-level citation grounding), making their exclusion especially warranted. Not outdated, not marketing fluff, not an overreach.

### [13] The names Elicit, Scholarcy, Scite, and Semantic Scholar are already in use by established AI-powered literature-review tools, confirming they are taken in the AI-research naming space and must be excluded.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.readwonders.com/blog/best-literature-review-tools-2026-ai-vs-traditional (blog)
Quote: "Elicit — uses language models to find and understand papers ... Scholarcy — AI-generated summary flashcards ... Scite — shows how papers have been cited ... Semantic Scholar — free, AI-powered search engine from the Allen Institute for AI"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is fully supported and independently corroborated by primary sources. All four names have live official product sites as AI-powered research/literature tools: Elicit (elicit.com — "AI for scientific research," searches 125M+ papers, generates literature reviews), Scite (scite.ai — AI research assistant grounded in 281M+ articles with Smart Citations), Semantic Scholar (semanticscholar.org — free AI-powered research tool from the Allen Institute for AI), and Scholarcy (scholarcy.com — AI summarization/flashcards, 600K+ users). These are backed not just by the cited blog but by multiple independent 2026-dated university library guides (Arizona, Oregon State, McMaster, Coastal Carolina) and peer-reviewed/medRxiv evaluations. The names are unambiguously in active use in the AI-research space and must be excluded from consideration — exactly as the claim states. No contradicting or qualifying evidence found; source strength exceeds the modest strength of the claim.

### [14] USPTO's TESS trademark search system was retired on November 30, 2023 and can no longer be used to search US trademarks, so the research task's instruction to 'search USPTO TESS' must be redirected to the successor system.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://signa.so/blog/tess-is-gone-how-to-search-trademarks (blog)
Quote: "The TESS system was retired on November 30, 2023, after 23 years of operation."
Verifier evidence (high): CONFIRMED by USPTO primary source (uspto.gov): "we will be retiring the Trademark Electronic Search System (TESS) on Thursday, November 30" [2023]. TESS is no longer operational; the successor is the cloud-based "Trademark Search" system at https://tmsearch.uspto.gov/ (Basic + Advanced modes). Multiple independent sources (Alt Legal, INTA, Renner Otto, Morse Law) corroborate the November 30, 2023 retirement date. The blog's supporting quote ("retired on November 30, 2023, after 23 years of operation") matches the government record exactly. Claim is current, well-supported, and backed by a primary source — the blog is merely secondary confirmation. Minor caveat: the same US trademark DATA remains fully searchable via the new interface, so the task's substantive goal (checking US TM availability) is unaffected — but the specific instruction to use "TESS" is indeed outdated and must be redirected to tmsearch.uspto.gov, exactly as the claim states.

### [15] US trademark searches (for verifying candidate brand names against live marks) must now be performed at the USPTO's cloud-based Trademark Search system at tmsearch.uspto.gov, which replaced TESS.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://signa.so/blog/tess-is-gone-how-to-search-trademarks (blog)
Quote: "The USPTO replaced TESS with a cloud-based system called Trademark Search, accessible at tmsearch.uspto.gov."
Verifier evidence (high): CONFIRMED by USPTO's own primary source. The official USPTO subscription-center page states: "we will be retiring the Trademark Electronic Search System (TESS) on Thursday, November 30. TESS will be replaced by our new trademark search system" — the replacement being the cloud-based system at tmsearch.uspto.gov (retirement date: November 30, 2023). Old TESS URLs now redirect to the new system. Multiple independent sources corroborate (USPTO.gov, Alt Legal, BOAG Law). The system is operational as of July 2026. The blog's quote is not an overreach; it accurately reflects the primary source. Claim is current, well-supported, and matches a primary-source-backed reality. One minor caveat that does NOT refute the claim: the USPTO Trademark Search covers only US filings, so EU/EUIPO checks (which the research task also requires) require a separate tool — but that is consistent with, not contradictory to, the claim.

### [16] Abstract/coined brand names are easier to trademark and legally protect because they are invented and unlikely to resemble existing names.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://fabrikbrands.com/branding-matters/brand-naming/what-are-abstract-brand-names/ (blog)
Quote: "you're not likely to end up with something similar to an existing title... you'll be able to trademark your moniker too, so it's easier to protect."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim reflects settled U.S. trademark doctrine (the distinctiveness spectrum), not blog fluff. Justia: fanciful/coined marks "represent the strongest category of trademarks... do not require proof of secondary meaning." INTA and multiple IP firms (Morse, Cohen IP, Crown LLP) confirm coined marks are the easiest to register and receive the broadest protection. The blog quote is corroborated by these primary/authoritative legal sources, so source quality is sufficient. Timeless legal principle, not outdated. One qualification: invented names are NOT fully immune — they can still resemble a mark and trigger a likelihood-of-confusion dispute (Fabrik itself, IndieLaw, PatentPC note searches remain essential). But the claim's core ("easier to trademark and legally protect") is accurate and well-grounded; the "unlikely to resemble existing names" part is a reasonable generalization, not a refuting error.

### [17] The USPTO has replaced the legacy TESS system with a new Trademark Search system, hosted at tmsearch.uspto.gov, which is the current official tool for searching the U.S. federal trademark database.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.uspto.gov/trademarks/search (primary)
Quote: "The system is called the "Trademark Search system" and accessible at https://tmsearch.uspto.gov/ ... it replaces the previous TESS system. The page references a "Transitioning from TESS handout""
Verifier evidence (high): Verified against primary and independent sources. USPTO's own pages ("Retiring TESS: What to know about the new trademark search system" and the "Introducing the USPTO's new cloud-based trademark search system" news update) confirm TESS was retired on Nov 30, 2023 and replaced by the "Trademark Search" system at tmsearch.uspto.gov. The uspto.gov/trademarks/search page itself links a "Transitioning from TESS handout" (TM-Transitioning-from-TESS-handout.pdf). Independent IP-law sources corroborate (Alt Legal, Renner Otto, Morse Law, Rutgers libguides), noting old TESS URLs now redirect to the new system. Source quality (primary .gov) matches claim strength; claim is current, not marketing, not outdated. No contradicting evidence found.

### [18] The USPTO advises running a comprehensive clearance search for similar trademarks before adopting a name, which is the recommended method for assessing trademark risk of a candidate brand name.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.uspto.gov/trademarks/search (primary)
Quote: "The page emphasizes conducting a "comprehensive clearance search for similar trademarks""
Verifier evidence (high): USPTO's live site directly supports the claim. The primary source page (uspto.gov/trademarks/search) links a dedicated resource titled "Comprehensive clearance search for similar trademarks" (uspto.gov/trademarks/search/comprehensive-clearance-search-similar-trademarks) and a "Why search for similar trademarks?" page (uspto.gov/trademarks/basics/why-search-similar-trademarks), confirming USPTO recommends a clearance search for similar marks before adopting a name. Independently corroborated by multiple law firms (Cranfill Sumner, Nelson Mullins, grant.legal), all describing a comprehensive clearance search as the recommended pre-adoption risk-assessment method. Claim is faithful to the quote, current, and sourced primarily. Minor nuance (not refuting): USPTO's recommended clearance search is broader than a bare USPTO-database/TESS search, covering state/common-law/phonetic conflicts — so it is the recommended method, but a database-only search is a narrower subset.

### [19] The service provides WHOIS registration data and domain history via its Domain Directory feature.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://instantdomainsearch.com/ (primary)
Quote: "WHOIS registration data and domain history"
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source instantdomainsearch.com confirms the claim directly. WebFetch of the site verifies it offers a "Domain Directory" feature whose per-domain directory pages (format instantdomainsearch.com/directory/{domain}) include "WHOIS registration data and domain history," alongside a dedicated WHOIS lookup tool (instantdomainsearch.com/whois-lookup). The supporting quote "WHOIS registration data and domain history" matches the source verbatim. This is a mundane, current, self-descriptive product-feature claim from the vendor's own primary site, not an extraordinary claim needing third-party corroboration; source quality matches claim strength. Only caveat: the claim is tangential to the naming research question, but as stated it is accurate and supported.

### [20] It supports bulk checking of up to 5,000 domains at once, useful for evaluating many candidate brand names.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://instantdomainsearch.com/ (primary)
Quote: "Bulk checking capability for up to 5,000 domains"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly supported by the primary source. Instant Domain Search has a dedicated page titled "Bulk Domain Search — Check 5,000 Domains at Once (Free)" (https://instantdomainsearch.com/bulk-domain-search), and the homepage's Bulk Search feature states you can "Check up to 5,000 domains with CSV import/export and multi-registrar pricing." An announcement post ("Introducing Bulk Domain Search") corroborates it. The 5,000 figure matches the quote exactly — no overreach. The feature is genuinely useful for evaluating many candidate brand names, which is exactly the deliverable's task. Source is the vendor's own current site (appropriate primary source for a product-capability claim, not an extraordinary factual claim needing third-party corroboration). No contradicting evidence found; not outdated (it's a live, currently-advertised feature).

### [21] The 'ver' root generates a family of English truth-words usable as naming inspiration, including 'verify', 'veracity', 'verity', 'verdict', 'veritable', 'veracious', 'verisimilitude', and 'aver'.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://membean.com/roots/ver-truth (secondary)
Quote: "This root is the word origin of a fair number of English vocabulary words, including ver dict and ver acity."
Verifier evidence (high): All eight cited words are standard derivatives of Latin 'verus' (true). Confirmed across independent sources: Membean and Wordpandit both list verify, veracity, verdict, aver, verisimilitude from root 'ver'; etymonline confirms verity/veritable (from veritas) and veracious/veracity (from verax) share the same root. The source's 'including verdict and veracity' is an explicit non-exhaustive example list ('a fair number... including'), so the fuller enumeration in the claim is a faithful extension, not an overreach. This is basic, verifiable etymology — no extraordinary-claim burden, not outdated, not marketing.

### [22] 'Veracity' specifically denotes truthfulness, and 'verity' denotes the truth or reality of something -- both short, brandable coinage candidates tied to the rigor positioning.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://membean.com/roots/ver-truth (secondary)
Quote: "veracity : ‘truthfulness’ ... verity : The ver ity of something is the truth or reality of it."
Verifier evidence (high): The core factual claim (word meanings) is confirmed by multiple authoritative sources. Merriam-Webster and Etymonline define "veracity" as "habitual truthfulness; honesty" (from Latin verax "truthful," from verus "true"). "Verity" is defined as "the quality of being true" / the truth or reality of something, same Latin root verus. This exactly matches the Membean quote and the claim's assertions. Both are short (3-syllable / 3-syllable), pronounceable, and semantically tied to truth/rigor positioning — supporting the branding rationale. Minor imprecision: they are established English words, not literal "coinages," but that does not undermine the load-bearing meaning claim, which is well-supported by primary-quality dictionary sources.

### [23] Veritas derives from Latin 'verus' ('true'), from PIE root *were-o- ('true, trustworthy'), which is the source of English words like verify, veracity, verdict, and verity — indicating a rich family of related brandable/coinable roots (e.g. Verus, Verity, Verify).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.etymonline.com/word/veritas (secondary)
Quote: "It derives from the Latin word *verus* meaning "true," which traces back to the Proto-Indo-European root *were-o- meaning "true, trustworthy.""
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported and non-controversial. Etymonline (cited source) explicitly derives veritas < verus "true" < PIE *were-o- "true, trustworthy" and lists verify, veracity, verdict, verity as same-root derivatives. Independently corroborated by Wiktionary (verus < Proto-Italic *wēros < PIE), which lists the same English family: aver, veracious, veracity, verdict, verify, verisimilitude, veritas, verity, very. Only minor caveat: modern scholarship reconstructs the root as *weh₁ros vs. Etymonline's older *were-o- notation — a notational difference, not a factual dispute; both mean "true, trustworthy." The claim's factual core (rich brandable/coinable root family: Verus, Verity, Verify) is accurate. Source quality (etymological dictionaries) matches the modest strength of this well-established etymology claim.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "A registered/parked .ai domain can be acquired via direct owner negotiation through escrow, domain brokers, aftermarket platforms like Sedo and Afternic, or the .ai registry's own expired-domain auction at auction.whois.ai" (https://www.onlydomains.com/blog/who-owns-a-ai-domain/, vote 0-3)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll synthesize the verified claims into a structured research report. Note that the surviving claims cover the research methodology and tooling (domain checkers, trademark systems, naming roots, excluded competitor names) but do not include the actual per-candidate availability checks (GitHub, TESS results, domain lookups, social handles) that the deliverable requires. I'll synthesize what was verified and flag this gap clearly.

input.summary:
The verified claim set establishes the research infrastructure and constraints for the naming task but does NOT contain completed per-candidate availability checks, so no fully-verified ranked shortlist of specific brand names can be delivered from these claims alone. What is confirmed: (1) the crowded competitor field to exclude — Elicit, Consensus, Scite, Perplexity, Semantic Scholar, Undermind, Scholarcy, SciSpace, STORM, plus adjacent tools Connected Papers, Research Rabbit, Litmaps, Iris.ai, Cypris, Paper Digest, Publish or Perish, and CORE; (2) the semantic naming direction with the strongest evidentiary support is the Latin 'ver-' truth-root family (verus/veritas), which spawns brandable candidates like Verus, Verity, Verify, Veracity, plus the Old English 'sooth' (truth); (3) the correct verification tooling and channels — USPTO's TESS was retired Nov 30 2023 and trademark checks must now run at tmsearch.uspto.gov (Nice classes 9 for downloadable software and 42 for SaaS/research services), and domain availability across .ai/.com can be checked in real time (including bulk-checking up to 5,000 candidates) via Instant Domain Search; and (4) abstract/coined names are the most legally ownable per USPTO distinctiveness doctrine. The actual GitHub-org, live-trademark, domain-registration, and X-handle results for named finalists were NOT among the surviving claims and remain open.</summary>
<parameter name="findings">[{"claim": "The AI deep-research/literature space is crowded, and a large set of names is already taken and must be excluded: Elicit, Consensus, Scite, Perplexity, Semantic Scholar, Undermind, Scholarcy, SciSpace, and STORM (Stanford) as core competitors, plus the adjacent tools Connected Papers, Research Rabbit, Litmaps, Iris.ai, Cypris, Paper Digest, Publish or Perish, and CORE.", "confidence": "high", "sources": ["https://agentic.ai/best/research-deep-analysis", "https://paperguide.ai/blog/ai-tools-for-research/", "https://paperguide.ai/blog/ai-tools-for-literature-review/", "https://www.editage.us/blog/best-ai-research-assistants/", "https://www.readwonders.com/blog/best-literature-review-tools-2026-ai-vs-traditional", "https://www.cypris.ai/insights/11-best-ai-tools-for-scientific-literature-review-in-2026"], "evidence": "Six independent 2026-dated sources (plus corroborating university library guides and primary product sites) confirm all of these are live, actively-marketed named products in the target/adjacent space. Consensus and Scite are functional near-twins of the product being named (claim-level citation grounding, 'no hallucination' positioning), making their exclusion especially warranted. Multiple claims (5,8,9,10,11,12,13) converge on this exclusion set with 3-0 votes.", "vote": "3-0"}, {"claim": "The Latin root 'ver-' (from verus 'true', from PIE *were-o- 'true, trustworthy') is the strongest-evidenced semantic direction for this faithfulness/rigor positioning, generating a family of brandable candidates: Verus, Verity, Verify, Veracity, Veritas, plus related forms (verdict, veritable, veracious, verisimilitude, aver).", "confidence": "high", "sources": ["https://membean.com/roots/ver-truth", "https://www.etymonline.com/word/veritas"], "evidence": "Etymonline, Wiktionary, and Merriam-Webster independently confirm verus 'true' as the root and enumerate the same derivative family. 'Veracity' = habitual truthfulness; 'verity' = the truth/reality of something — both short (3-syllable), pronounceable, and directly tied to the truth/faithfulness positioning. CAVEAT: 'Veritas' itself is heavily used commercially (e.g. Veritas Technologies backup software, Harvard's motto) and would likely be RED on trademark; the coined/abstract shortenings (Verus, Verity) are safer per the distinctiveness doctrine but still require live-mark clearance. Claims 2,3,21,22,23 support the root; they do NOT clear any specific name.", "vote": "3-0"}, {"claim": "The Old English root 'sooth' (soð = 'truth, reality, fact; true, genuine, real') is a secondary but valid truth-themed naming source.", "confidence": "high", "sources": ["https://www.etymonline.com/word/sooth"], "evidence": "Etymonline and Wiktionary confirm soð 'truth' from Proto-Germanic *sanþaz. CAVEAT flagged by verifier: 'sooth' is archaic and survives mainly in 'soothsayer,' which connotes fortune-telling/prophecy rather than rigorous verified truth — a positioning risk for a faithfulness-focused product.", "vote": "3-0"}, {"claim": "Trademark clearance for candidate names must be run at the USPTO's cloud-based Trademark Search system (tmsearch.uspto.gov), NOT the legacy TESS system, which was retired November 30, 2023; searches should cover Nice Class 9 (downloadable software) and Class 42 (SaaS / scientific-and-technological research services).", "confidence": "high", "sources": ["https://www.uspto.gov/trademarks/search", "https://signa.so/blog/tess-is-gone-how-to-search-trademarks", "https://corsearch.com/content-library/blog/international-trademark-classes/"], "evidence": "USPTO's own primary pages confirm TESS retirement (Nov 30 2023) and replacement by tmsearch.uspto.gov; old TESS URLs redirect. WIPO/USPTO Nice headings confirm Class 9 covers downloadable computer software and Class 42 covers SaaS + scientific/technological research services — both relevant for this product. USPTO recommends a comprehensive clearance search (broader than a database-only check, covering state/common-law/phonetic conflicts) before adopting a name. Claims 6,7,14,15,17,18 all 3-0. NOTE: this is METHODOLOGY — no actual mark searches for finalists were performed in the verified claim set. EUIPO (EU) requires a separate tool.", "vote": "3-0"}, {"claim": "Domain availability across .ai and .com (and 800+ TLDs) can be verified in real time via Instant Domain Search, including bulk-checking up to 5,000 candidate names at once, with WHOIS/registration-history data available.", "confidence": "high", "sources": ["https://instantdomainsearch.com/"], "evidence": "Primary vendor source confirms real-time as-you-type checking across 800+ TLDs, dedicated per-TLD pages for both .ai and .com (990,913 registered .ai domains as of 2026), a bulk tool for up to 5,000 domains with CSV import/export and multi-registrar pricing, and a Domain Directory with WHOIS data. Claims 0,1,19,20 all 3-0. This is the tool to RUN the checks; it does not itself contain results for any specific candidate.", "vote": "3-0"}, {"claim": "Abstract/coined (fanciful) brand names are the most legally ownable and easiest to protect, per USPTO trademark distinctiveness doctrine — supporting a preference for invented names (Vercel/Cohere/Anthropic-style) over descriptive or common real words.", "confidence": "high", "sources": ["https://fabrikbrands.com/branding-matters/brand-naming/what-are-abstract-brand-names/"], "evidence": "Corroborated by Justia, INTA, and multiple IP firms: fanciful/coined marks are the strongest category, require no proof of secondary meaning, and receive the broadest protection. QUALIFICATION: coined names are not immune to likelihood-of-confusion disputes, so a clearance search remains essential even for invented words.", "vote": "3-0"}]
input.caveats: CRITICAL GAP: The 24 surviving claims establish the research SCAFFOLDING (exclusion list, semantic direction, verification tools, legal doctrine) but contain ZERO completed per-candidate availability checks. None of the claims report a specific finalist name with its actual GitHub-org status, live-trademark result, .ai/.com registration status, or @handle availability. Therefore the requested deliverable — a ranked 8-12 name shortlist with GREEN/YELLOW/RED flags per channel and a TOP RECOMMENDATION — CANNOT be produced from this verified evidence alone; doing so would require fabricating availability results, which violates the faithfulness mandate. The candidate names surfaced (Verus, Verity, Verify, Veracity) are semantic suggestions derived from the verified 'ver-' root, NOT cleared names. Additional caveats: (1) 'Veritas' and likely several ver-derivatives are heavily trademarked and would fail clearance. (2) Several exclusion-list sources are blog-quality listicles, though independently corroborated by primary product sites and university library guides. (3) All availability data is time-sensitive — domain, trademark, and handle status change daily and must be re-checked at point of decision. (4) One claim was REFUTED (0-3): the specifics of how a parked .ai domain can be acquired (escrow/Sedo/Afternic/registry auction) did not survive verification, so acquisition-feasibility notes for taken .ai domains are unsupported. (5) EUIPO/EU trademark checks require a separate tool from tmsearch.uspto.gov and were not addressed by any surviving claim.
openQuestions: For each proposed candidate (e.g. Verus, Verity, Verify, and any coined alternatives), what are the ACTUAL results of the four availability checks — GitHub org, live trademark in Nice classes 9/42 at tmsearch.uspto.gov and EUIPO, .ai/.com registration status, and @handle on X — that the deliverable requires but that no surviving claim provides?

Which specific coined/abstract names (beyond real ver-words) best fit the positioning while being GREEN across all channels? The verified evidence favors invented names for ownability but supplies no cleared coined candidates.

Are any of the ver-root real words (Verus, Verity, Verify, Veracity) already taken as live software/AI trademarks or product names — several appear commercially common and likely YELLOW/RED, but no verified check was run?
What is the correct, verified process for checking and (if needed) acquiring EU trademarks and parked .ai domains, given the .ai-acquisition claim was refuted and EUIPO was never covered?
